1. How to Use This Book This book is designed to help you learn statistics with *jamovi*, a free statistical software program. My goal is not for you to memorize formulas or become a mathematician. My goal is for you to learn how to use statistics to answer research questions, interpret results, and explain what those results mean. If you are feeling nervous about statistics, you are not alone. Many students come into this course feeling unsure, anxious, or convinced that they are “not a stats person.” That is okay. Statistics is a skill, and skills develop with practice, feedback, and patience. This chapter explains how to use the book, what the book assumes, how to navigate the online version, and how to get help when you need it. 1.1 What This Book Is Trying to Help You Do By the end of this book, you should be better able to: understand why a particular statistical test fits a research question use *jamovi* to run statistical analyses read statistical output without getting overwhelmed interpret results in plain language write up results clearly and accurately make thoughtful decisions about data, assumptions, and evidence 1.3 Common Challenges (and How to Handle Them) “I don’t understand the math.” That is okay. There is some math in this book because formulas can sometimes help us understand what a statistic is doing. However, this is not a course where the main goal is hand calculations. Most of the time, I care much more about whether you understand what the statistic means, why you chose it, and how to interpret the results. “I got lost in jamovi.” This happens, especially when you are new to statistical software. Slow down, go back to the previous step, and compare your screen to the example. A lot of learning *jamovi* is simply repetition. The more you practice opening data, checking variables, selecting analyses, and reading output, the more familiar it will become. “I don’t know what the output means.” This is one of the most common challenges in statistics. When you look at output, start with the research question. Ask yourself: What was I trying to find out? What test did I run? Which part of the output helps me answer the question? Then use the interpretation sections in this book to help connect the statistical results back to the research question. 1.4 Navigating This Book This book is meant to be used as an online resource, not just read straight through from beginning to end. You will likely move back and forth between chapters, especially once you start working on assignments in jamovi. A few features may help: Use the sidebar/table of contents to jump between chapters and sections. Use the search feature when you are trying to find a specific term, test, or jamovi step. Use the previous/next buttons when you are working through chapters in order. Adjust the font size or display settings if that makes the book easier to read. My recommendation is to keep this book open while you work in jamovi. When you get stuck, search the book first, then go back to the relevant section and compare your screen to the example. 1.5 Technology Tips You will need access to a computer that can run jamovi and open common file types such as Word, Excel, PDF, and data files. I strongly recommend using the desktop version of jamovi when possible. jamovi Cloud exists, but the free version may not be sufficient for everything you need to do. The desktop version is free and is the version this book assumes you are using unless otherwise noted. You will often be moving back and forth between this book, assignment instructions, datasets, and jamovi. If you have two monitors, this is a great course to use them. If not, learn how to snap windows side-by-side or switch quickly between open applications. On Windows, Alt+Tab lets you move between open applications. On Mac, use Cmd+Tab. One very practical tip: close what you are not using. When you are working on statistics, try to have only the book, the assignment, your data file, and jamovi open. It reduces visual clutter, helps your computer run better, and makes it easier to focus. Also, restart your computer regularly. I know this sounds like basic technology advice, but many mysterious software problems are solved by saving your work, closing programs, restarting your computer, and trying again. And sometimes that breather can help you better solve problems! 1.6 Getting Help Learning statistics is not something most people do perfectly the first time. Getting stuck is part of the process. If you are in one of my courses, check Canvas for the current options for getting help. Depending on the semester, this may include student hours, Teams, email, class discussion spaces, or support from a teaching assistant or graduate assistant. When you ask for help, it is useful to include: the chapter or assignment you are working on what step you got stuck on what you already tried a screenshot of what you are seeing in jamovi, if relevant your jamovi file, if you are asking about an analysis You do not need to have a perfectly formed question before asking for help. “I got lost around this step” is a completely reasonable place to start. 1.7 A Final Tip One final thing to keep in mind: most mistakes in statistics are not signs that you “can’t do stats.” They are usually small process errors: the wrong variable type, the wrong box in jamovi, a missed assumption check, or an interpretation that moved too quickly. Slow down. Check your setup. Ask what the research question is. Then ask what the statistic is helping you decide. That process will take you much further than memorizing isolated rules. 1.8 Errors, Mistakes, and Suggestions I am human, therefore I err. If you find an error in this book, something that seems unclear, or something that does not match what you are seeing in *jamovi*, please let me know. It is especially helpful if you tell me the chapter or section, what seems wrong or confusing, and what you think might need to be updated. I also welcome suggestions. This book is meant to be useful to students in this course and to anyone else learning statistics with *jamovi*. If something would make the book clearer, more accessible, or easier to use, I would genuinely like to know.

2. Statistics foundations This chapter introduces some of the statistical language we will use throughout the book. You do not need to memorize every term right now. Instead, focus on understanding the big ideas: what data are, what kinds of variables we work with, how we describe data, and how we use samples to make inferences about populations. You will come back to these ideas again and again. If something does not fully click the first time through, that is okay. The goal here is to build a foundation we can keep returning to. As you read, pay special attention to the examples. In statistics, the same word can mean different things depending on how something is measured. For example, “time” might be categorical in one study and continuous in another. That is why I want you to focus less on memorizing definitions and more on asking: What does this variable actually represent? By the end of this chapter, you should be able to: explain how data are used to answer research questions recognize how datasets are organized into rows and columns distinguish among nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio variables explain the difference between descriptive and inferential statistics use key statistical terms that will appear throughout the textbook 2.1 Describing Data: Introduces what data are, how we summarize them, and why descriptive statistics matter. 2.2 Types of Variables and Levels of Measurement: Explains different types of variables and how they influence the summaries, graphs, and analyses we choose. 2.3 Descriptive vs Inferential Statistics: Builds the conceptual foundation for sampling, generalization, and statistical inference. 2.4 Key Terms and Concepts: Provides definitions and explanations of statistical terms you will encounter throughout the textbook. You do not need to memorize all of these now. Return to this section as needed. 2.5 Putting It All Together: Pulls the chapter together and gives you practice applying the main ideas. These ideas matter because they help you avoid some of the most common mistakes in statistics. Before you can run a statistical test, you need to know what kind of variables you have. Before you interpret a result, you need to know whether you are describing your sample or making an inference about a population. Before you report a mean, you need to know whether a mean makes sense for that variable. These may seem like small details, but they shape almost every decision you make when analyzing data.

2.1 Describing data By the end of this section, you should be able to: explain what data are and why context matters identify observations and variables in a dataset recognize features of a well-organized dataset explain what it means to describe data identify the main features we look for in a distribution Before we can analyze data, we need to understand what our data represent. At its core, statistics is about using data to answer questions. In most research settings, data are organized in a dataset, often in a spreadsheet format. Each row usually represents one observation, such as one participant, and each column represents one variable, such as age, condition, or test score. Statistical software, including jamovi, expects data to be organized in a clear and consistent way. Here is the basic structure to keep in mind: Rows represent observations, cases, or participants. Columns represent variables. Cells contain one value for one observation on one variable. A value by itself does not mean much. For example, 75 could be a test score, heart rate, temperature, percentile rank, or something else entirely. To interpret data correctly, we need to know what each variable represents, how it was measured, and what the values mean. This is why clear variable names and documentation are important when working with datasets. In formal scientific writing, including APA style, data is usually treated as plural: “The data are consistent with the hypothesis.” In everyday speech, many people use data as singular: “The data is stored in a spreadsheet.” You will see both. In your formal research writing, use the plural form unless your instructor or style guide says otherwise. A well-organized dataset usually has one row for each observation, one column for each variable, one value in each cell, clear variable names, and clearly coded missing data. jamovi knows to treat blank cells as missing, but in many other contexts you may see missing values coded as NA or another clearly documented value. Good organization helps prevent errors and makes data easier to analyze, share, and reproduce (Broman & Woo, 2018). A variable is anything we measure or record that can vary across individuals or observations. Examples include age, major, test score, treatment condition, or survey response. Variables can be measured in different ways, and those differences matter. The next section explains levels of measurement, which help us decide how to summarize variables and what analyses are appropriate. Once we collect data, the first step is to describe what we have. Raw data can be difficult to interpret on their own, especially when we have many rows. Descriptive statistics and visualizations help us summarize the data, identify patterns, notice unusual values, and prepare for further analysis. When we describe data, we usually focus on four features: Center: What is typical? Common measures include the mean, median, and mode. These are called measures of central tendency. Variability: How spread out are the values? Common measures include the range, interquartile range, variance, and standard deviation. These are called measures of dispersion. Shape: What does the distribution look like? For example, is it approximately normal, skewed in one direction, or shaped in some other way? Outliers: Are there unusually high or low values that need closer attention? A distribution shows how values in a dataset are spread out and how often they occur. Instead of looking at one score at a time, a distribution helps us see the whole picture: where values cluster, how spread out they are, whether the data are symmetrical or skewed, and whether any values look unusual. One particular distribution we care a lot about in psychological research is the normal distribution, sometimes called a bell curve. In a normal distribution, most values cluster around the center, fewer values appear as you move away from the center, and the left and right sides are approximately symmetrical. Many statistical methods assume that data are approximately normally distributed, which is why we return to this idea often. Not all data are normally distributed, though. In a skewed distribution, one side of the distribution stretches out more than the other. A right-skewed or positively skewed distribution has a tail that extends to the right. A left-skewed or negatively skewed distribution has a tail that extends to the left. Kurtosis is another aspect of shape that describes how heavy or light the tails of a distribution are. You do not need to master skew and kurtosis yet, but you should know that distributions can differ in center, variability, shape, and outliers. Describing data is not busywork. It is how we catch problems, understand what our data actually look like, and avoid jumping too quickly into statistical tests. Before you interpret any inferential test, you should have some sense of the sample, variables, distributions, and possible unusual values. Here is a video walking through some of the ideas in this chapter. In a dataset, what does each row represent? What does each column represent? Why do data need context to be meaningful? What are two features of a well-organized dataset? What are the four main features we often examine when describing data? Why can two datasets with the same mean still be very different? Rows represent observations, cases, or participants. Columns represent variables. A value does not mean much by itself. We need to know what the variable represents, how it was measured, and what the values mean. Answers will vary. Examples include one row per observation, one column per variable, one value per cell, clear variable names, and clearly coded missing data. Center, variability, shape, and outliers. They may have different variability, different shapes, or different outliers.
2.2 Types of variables and levels of measurement

2.2 Types of variables and levels of measurement By the end of this section, you should be able to: distinguish among nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio variables explain why a variable’s level of measurement depends on how it is measured identify whether a variable is categorical or continuous for the purposes of analysis explain why assigning numbers to categories does not make a variable continuous explain why collapsing variables can reduce information In Section 2.1, we introduced variables as things we measure or record that can vary across observations. Now we need to get more specific. Variables can be measured at different levels of measurement, and those levels help us decide what kinds of summaries, graphs, and analyses are appropriate. Broadly, variables are either categorical or continuous. Categorical variables describe groups or categories. Continuous variables represent quantities or amounts. We can refine those broad categories into four levels of measurement: nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio. Nominal variables are categorical variables with no inherent order. Examples include major, treatment group, eye color, type of pet, or whether someone is an athlete. A special type of nominal variable has only two categories, such as yes/no or present/absent. These are often called dichotomous, binary, or dummy-coded variables. With nominal variables, we can count how many observations fall into each category and calculate percentages. We cannot meaningfully order or average the values. If I code cat = 1, dog = 2, and frog = 3, that does not mean dogs are “more” than cats or that frogs are three times cats. The numbers are just labels. Ordinal variables are categorical variables with a meaningful order, but the distances between the categories are not necessarily equal. Examples include class rank, satisfaction ratings, and Likert-scale responses such as strongly disagree to strongly agree. With ordinal variables, we can order values and often use medians, ranges, frequencies, and percentages. However, we should be cautious about assuming the distance between “strongly disagree” and “disagree” is exactly the same as the distance between “agree” and “strongly agree.” Interval variables are continuous variables with equal spacing between values, but no true zero point. Temperature in Celsius or Fahrenheit is a classic example. The difference between 20 and 30 degrees is the same size as the difference between 30 and 40 degrees, but 0 degrees does not mean there is no temperature. Because there is no true zero, ratio statements are not meaningful. We would not say that 40°F is twice as warm as 20°F. Ratio variables are continuous variables with equal spacing between values and a true zero point. Examples include reaction time, number of correct responses, time spent studying, height, weight, and frequency of a behavior. Because ratio variables have a true zero, we can make meaningful ratio statements, such as “twice as long” or “half as many.” In this course, I will usually ask you to distinguish among nominal, ordinal, and continuous variables. You should understand the difference between interval and ratio variables, but most of the analyses we use in jamovi treat both as continuous. Some quantitative variables are continuous, while others are discrete counts. For example, reaction time can take on many possible decimal values, whereas number of errors is counted in whole numbers. In practice, jamovi often treats both as continuous variables, and we will usually do the same in this course. A simple way to identify variable type is to ask three questions: Is this variable describing categories or numbers? If it describes categories, is there a meaningful order? If yes, it is ordinal. If no, it is nominal. If it describes numbers, does zero represent none of the thing being measured? If yes, it is ratio. If no, it is interval. The most important thing to remember is this: a variable’s level of measurement depends on how it is measured, not just what it is. One semester, a student asked, “Isn’t time a continuous variable?” The answer is: it depends. If time is measured as pre vs. post, it is a nominal variable with two categories. If time is measured as month of the year, it may be treated as ordinal in some contexts because the months have a meaningful sequence, although months can also be cyclical depending on the research question. If time is measured as response time, exact date, or time remaining, it is typically continuous. So “time” is not automatically one type of variable. Its level of measurement depends on the way the researcher defines and records it. Here is another example. The construct studying could be measured in different ways. Name Study_Continuous Study_Ordinal Study_Nominal Name (Character) Hours studied per day Amount of studying Studies every day? Jesus 5.0 A great deal Yes Nicky 4.5 A great deal Yes Bradford 3.2 A moderate amount Yes Sylvia 1.7 A small amount Yes Martha 0.2 Rarely Yes Lillian 0.0 Never No Trayvon 0.0 Never No Notice that the same general idea can be measured as continuous, ordinal, or nominal. Hours studied per day is continuous. Amount of studying on an ordered scale is ordinal. Whether someone studies every day is nominal. This matters because different ways of measuring a variable preserve different amounts of information. In general, moving from continuous → ordinal → nominal reduces information. If we turn hours studied into low, medium, and high, we lose the exact differences between people. If we turn hours studied into “studied every day” versus “did not study every day,” we lose even more information. For that reason, researchers usually want to avoid collapsing variables into categories unless there is a strong reason to do so. A mean split or median split, for example, turns a continuous variable into categories such as “above average” and “below average.” This may seem convenient, but it removes information and can make analyses less precise. At the same time, “more information” does not always mean “better.” The best level of measurement depends on your research question and how the construct is most meaningfully measured. Variable type determines what descriptive statistics, graphs, and analyses are appropriate. If you treat a nominal variable as continuous, you might calculate a mean that does not mean anything. If you collapse a continuous variable into categories too quickly, you may throw away information you needed. Is “favorite color” a nominal, ordinal, interval, or ratio variable? Is a 1–5 satisfaction rating scale nominal or ordinal? Why? What makes ratio variables different from interval variables? Why can’t we calculate a meaningful average for nominal data? Give one example of each type of variable: nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio. Nominal. Ordinal, because the responses are ordered but the spacing between values is not guaranteed to be equal. Ratio variables have a true zero point; interval variables do not. Because the categories do not have numerical meaning or order. Answers will vary. For example, nominal: major; ordinal: class rank; interval: temperature in Celsius; ratio: height.

2.3 Descriptive vs inferential statistics By the end of this section, you should be able to: distinguish between descriptive and inferential statistics explain the difference between a sample and a population explain the difference between a statistic and a parameter describe why researchers often use samples to make inferences about populations identify when a claim is descriptive versus inferential There are two broad types of statistics that we use when working with data: descriptive statistics and inferential statistics. Descriptive statistics are used to summarize, organize, and describe the data we have collected. They focus on the sample data in front of us. Common descriptive statistics include measures of central tendency, measures of dispersion, descriptions of shape, and visualizations such as tables and graphs. Inferential statistics are used when we collect data from a sample and use those data to make inferences about a larger population. Common inferential statistics include t-tests, ANOVA, correlation, chi-square tests, and regression. A population is the full group we are interested in understanding. A sample is the smaller group we actually collect data from. A parameter describes a population, such as the true average test score of all students in a university. A statistic describes a sample, such as the average test score of the students in our study. In research, we often use statistics to estimate unknown population parameters. We typically use both descriptive and inferential statistics. We use descriptive statistics to summarize our sample, and we use inferential statistics to evaluate what the sample may suggest about the population. Some studies are purely descriptive, with no attempt to generalize beyond the collected data. However, when we conduct inferential statistics, we still begin by describing the sample. This can feel abstract, so let’s walk through a simple example. Imagine we are conducting a study to examine whether watching Schitt’s Creek (a very good show, but not helpful for studying) versus watching video lessons on studying techniques (useful, but perhaps less exciting) affects test performance in UW–Stout students. Our population of interest is all UW–Stout students. However, it is not feasible to collect data from every student due to time, access, and logistical constraints. Instead, we collect data from a sample. For simplicity, we use students from two sections of an introductory psychology course, about 80 students total. This is a convenience sample, not a random sample, but it works for illustrating our concepts. We randomly assign half of the students to watch Schitt’s Creek and the other half to watch study skills videos. One week later, all students take an exam, and we record their test scores. First, we use descriptive statistics to summarize the sample. We might create a histogram of test scores, calculate the mean score for each group, examine the standard deviation to understand variability, and check the range of scores. These summaries help us understand what the data look like. Next, we ask our research question: Which group performed better on the exam? To answer this, we use inferential statistics. We take our descriptive statistics, such as means, standard deviations, and sample sizes, and use a statistical test. In this case, we would use an independent samples t-test, which you will learn about later. Rather than calculating this by hand, we typically use statistical software like jamovi, which provides a test statistic, a p-value, and an effect size. These results help us evaluate whether the observed difference in our sample is larger than we would expect if there were no real difference in the population. Descriptive statistics tell us what happened in the data we collected. Inferential statistics help us decide what, if anything, those data suggest about a larger population. If we confuse the two, we can easily overstate what a study shows. You collect data from 50 UW–Stout students and calculate the mean GPA. Is this descriptive or inferential statistics? Explain briefly. You use those 50 students to test whether UW–Stout students have a higher GPA than the national average. Is this descriptive or inferential statistics? Explain briefly. A researcher reports that 62% of participants preferred online classes. What type of statistic is this? Why do we typically use both descriptive and inferential statistics in the same study? A study includes only descriptive statistics and no inferential tests. Give one reason why that might be appropriate. Descriptive. You are summarizing the sample you collected. Inferential. You are using the sample to make a claim about a larger population. Descriptive. It summarizes the sample data. Descriptive statistics summarize what the sample looks like, and inferential statistics help us evaluate what the sample may suggest about the population. Answers will vary. For example, the goal of the study may simply be to describe a group or summarize observations without making broader population claims.

2.4 Key terms and concepts By the end of this section, you should be able to: define key research design and statistical terms used throughout the textbook distinguish between independent and dependent variables recognize common study design terms explain reliability and validity at a basic level use this section as a reference when unfamiliar terms appear later This section introduces terms that will come up throughout the book. You do not need to memorize all of them now. Instead, use this section as a reference point when you encounter these terms later. Independent variable (IV): The variable that is thought to be the cause of some effect. In experimental research, it is the variable that is manipulated. It may also be called a predictor variable or exposure variable. Dependent variable (DV): The variable that is thought to be affected by the independent variable. It may also be called an outcome variable. In research, this is the variable we measure. For example, if we study whether watching study skills videos improves exam performance, the type of video is the independent variable and exam performance is the dependent variable. Confounding variable: A variable that affects or is related to both the independent variable and dependent variable. Confounds make it harder to interpret whether the IV is actually related to changes in the DV. Covariate: A variable that is related to the dependent variable and may be statistically controlled in an analysis. Covariates are not usually the main focus of the study. Between-subjects design: Participants are only exposed to one condition. In other words, there are different people in each condition. Within-subjects design: Participants are exposed to every condition. In other words, the same people appear in each condition. This may also be called a repeated-measures design. Cross-sectional research: Data are collected at one point in time. This is often non-experimental research because the independent variable is not manipulated and there is no random assignment. Longitudinal research: Data are collected from the same participants across multiple time points. Correlational research: A study in which variables are measured but not manipulated. Correlational research can tell us whether variables are related, but correlation does not establish causation. It is one necessary condition for causal inference, but it is not sufficient by itself. Experimental research: Research in which the independent variable is manipulated and participants are randomly assigned to conditions. Quasi-experimental research: Research in which the independent variable is manipulated or groups are compared, but there is no random assignment. Random assignment: Participants are randomly assigned to conditions. Random assignment helps reduce systematic differences between groups before the study begins. Hypothesis: A prediction about what we think the answer to our research question will be. In null hypothesis significance testing, we usually distinguish between the alternative hypothesis and the null hypothesis. Alternative hypothesis: The hypothesis that there is an effect, relationship, or difference. This is often what the researcher expects to find. Null hypothesis: The hypothesis that there is no effect, relationship, or difference. A special type of null hypothesis is the nil hypothesis, which states that the effect is exactly zero. Falsification: A key idea in science. We generally try to falsify hypotheses rather than prove them. In null hypothesis significance testing, we test against the null hypothesis; we do not prove the alternative hypothesis. Theory: A broader explanation of a behavior or phenomenon that can generate predictions about future observations. Qualitative methods: Methods that focus on words, meaning, interpretation, and context. Interviews, focus groups, and open-ended responses are common examples. Quantitative methods: Methods that focus on numerical data and statistical analysis. Surveys with numeric responses, test scores, and behavioral counts are common examples. Reliability: The consistency of a measure. A measure may be consistent across time, across items, or across raters. For example, a reliable scale should generally produce consistent results when the construct being measured has not changed. Validity: The extent to which evidence supports the interpretation and use of scores for a particular purpose. People often say validity means “measuring what you intend to measure,” which is a decent starting point, but validity is really about the quality of the inferences we make from the data. Some validity terms you may encounter include: Construct validity: Evidence that scores relate to the broader construct they are intended to represent. Content validity: Evidence that the measure adequately covers the content or domain of interest. Convergent validity: Evidence that scores are related to measures of theoretically similar constructs. Discriminant validity: Evidence that scores are not too strongly related to measures of theoretically different constructs. Criterion validity: Evidence that scores are related to an important outcome or criterion. Internal validity: Evidence that the observed relationship reflects a causal relationship rather than some other explanation. External validity: Evidence that findings generalize across people, settings, measures, or contexts. Statistical conclusion validity: Evidence that the statistical conclusions are appropriate given the design, measures, and analyses. Systematic variation: Variation that is introduced in a predictable or patterned way. In an experiment, we intentionally create systematic variation by assigning people to different conditions. However, systematic variation can also be unintentional, such as when one researcher is consistently warmer to participants than another researcher. Unsystematic variation: Random variation that is not patterned or predictable. Some amount of unsystematic variation is expected in research. Degrees of freedom (df): The number of values that are free to vary once certain constraints are in place. The easiest way to understand this is with a simple example. If four numbers must add up to 10, the first three numbers are free to vary, but the fourth number is constrained by the values of the first three. In that case, the degrees of freedom would be 3, or n - 1. Confidence interval: A range of plausible values for a population parameter. In repeated sampling, a 95% confidence interval procedure should produce intervals that contain the true population parameter about 95% of the time. We will return to confidence intervals later. Statistical tests are not just button-clicking procedures. They are connected to research design, measurement, sampling, and interpretation. These terms give us the language to explain what we did and what our results can and cannot tell us. In a study examining whether sleep affects exam performance, what might the IV and DV be? What is the difference between a between-subjects design and a within-subjects design? Why does correlation not establish causation? What is the difference between reliability and validity? What is one reason a confounding variable can make a study difficult to interpret? The IV might be amount of sleep or sleep condition. The DV might be exam performance. In a between-subjects design, different people are in each condition. In a within-subjects design, the same people are measured across conditions or time points. Because the variables are measured rather than manipulated, and other variables may explain the relationship. Reliability is consistency. Validity is about whether evidence supports the interpretation and use of scores. A confounding variable is related to both the IV and DV, so it can provide an alternative explanation for the results.

2.5 Putting it all together By the end of this section, you should be able to: connect variables, datasets, distributions, descriptive statistics, and inferential statistics identify appropriate descriptive summaries for different variable types explain why descriptive statistics come before inferential statistics apply the main ideas from the chapter to a simple dataset This chapter introduced the language we will use throughout the rest of the book. The most important idea is that statistical decisions depend on what your data represent. Before choosing a graph, calculating a statistic, or running a test in jamovi, ask: What is my research question? What are my variables? How is each variable measured? Am I describing my sample or making an inference about a population? What does the distribution of my data look like? If you can answer those questions, you are already doing a lot of the thinking that statistics requires. Here is a quick reference for the major variable types: Type Description Example Common summaries Nominal Categories with no order Major Counts, frequencies, percentages Ordinal Ordered categories Satisfaction ratings Frequencies, percentages, medians, ranges Continuous Numerical values with meaningful distance between values Test scores, reaction time Means, standard deviations, ranges, visual distributions And here is a quick reference for features of a distribution: Feature Question Why it matters Center What is typical? Helps describe the general location of the values Variability How spread out are values? Shows whether values are similar or very different Shape What pattern do values form? Helps us recognize skew, normality, and other patterns Outliers Are there unusual values? Can influence results and may need closer attention A few key ideas to carry forward: A variable’s level of measurement depends on how it is measured, not just what it represents. Assigning numbers to categories does not make a variable continuous. Collapsing variables into categories can reduce information. Descriptive statistics help us understand the data we collected. Inferential statistics help us evaluate what our sample may suggest about a population. We should look at our data before jumping to conclusions. In the next chapters, you will begin working directly with data in jamovi. You will learn how to open datasets, check variable types, describe variables numerically, visualize distributions, and prepare data for analysis. Each of those skills builds directly on the ideas in this chapter. A dataset includes the following variables: major, stress rating on a 1–5 scale, and hours of sleep last night. For each variable, identify whether it is categorical or continuous and state one appropriate way to describe it. Why is it important to correctly identify variable types before running analyses? A researcher reports only the mean for a variable. What important information might still be missing? Explain the difference between a statistic and a parameter. A variable is coded as 1 = cat, 2 = dog, and 3 = frog. Why is this still not a continuous variable? Major is categorical and could be described with frequencies or percentages. Stress rating is ordinal and could be described with frequencies, percentages, or a median. Hours of sleep is continuous and could be described with a mean and standard deviation. Variable type determines what descriptive statistics, graphs, and analyses are appropriate. If a variable is classified incorrectly, the results may be misleading. Variability, shape, range, outliers, and sample size may still be missing. The mean alone does not fully describe the data. A statistic describes a sample, while a parameter describes a population. We often use statistics to estimate parameters. Because the numbers are only labels for categories; they do not represent meaningful numerical quantities. Think about a dataset you might collect in your own field. What variables would you measure? Would they be categorical or continuous? What might the distribution of your data look like? What would you want to describe or compare? Taking a moment to think through these questions can help you connect these concepts to real-world data.

2. Statistics foundations This chapter introduces some of the statistical language we will use throughout the book. You do not need to memorize every term right now. Instead, focus on understanding the big ideas: what data are, what kinds of variables we work with, how we describe data, and how we use samples to make inferences about populations. You will come back to these ideas again and again. If something does not fully click the first time through, that is okay. The goal here is to build a foundation we can keep returning to. As you read, pay special attention to the examples. In statistics, the same word can mean different things depending on how something is measured. For example, “time” might be categorical in one study and continuous in another. That is why I want you to focus less on memorizing definitions and more on asking: What does this variable actually represent? What You’ll Learn By the end of this chapter, you should be able to: explain how data are used to answer research questions recognize how datasets are organized into rows and columns distinguish among nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio variables explain the difference between descriptive and inferential statistics use key statistical terms that will appear throughout the textbook Chapter Sections 2.1 Describing Our Data: Introduces what data are, how we summarize them, and why descriptive statistics matter. 2.2 Levels of Measurement: Explains different types of variables and how they influence the analyses we choose. 2.3 Descriptive vs Inferential Statistics: Builds the conceptual foundation for hypothesis testing and statistical inference. 2.4 Key Terms & Concepts: Provides definitions and explanations of statistical terms you will encounter throughout the textbook. You do not need to memorize all of these now—return to this section as needed. Why This Matters These ideas matter because they help you avoid some of the most common mistakes in statistics. For example, before you can run a statistical test, you need to know what kind of variables you have. Before you interpret a result, you need to know whether you are describing your sample or making an inference about a population. Before you report a mean, you need to know whether a mean makes sense for that variable. These may seem like small details, but they shape almost every decision you make when analyzing data.

2.1 Describing data Before we can analyze data, we need to understand what our data represent. At its core, statistics is about using data to answer questions. In most research settings, data are organized in a dataset, often in a spreadsheet format. Each row usually represents one observation, such as one participant, and each column represents one variable, such as age, condition, or test score. This structure matters because statistical software, including jamovi, expects data to be organized in a clear and consistent way. First, let’s understand some basic statistics related to how we describe our data, including measures of central tendency (averages), measures of dispersion (spread), and measures of shape of the distribution (particularly a normal distribution). Here’s a video walking you through what we learn in this chapter. Learning Objectives By the end of this section, you should be able to: Define what data are and explain why context matters Identify observations (rows) and variables (columns) in a dataset Distinguish between categorical and numerical variables Describe how datasets are structured for analysis Recognize features of a well-organized dataset What Are Data? In research, data are typically organized in a dataset (often a spreadsheet format), where: Rows represent individual observations (e.g., participants) Columns represent variables (e.g., age, test score, group membership) Each value in the dataset tells us something about a specific observation on a specific variable. Is “data” singular or plural? In formal scientific writing, including APA style, *data* is usually treated as plural: “The data are consistent with the hypothesis.” In everyday speech, many people use *data* as singular: “The data is stored in a spreadsheet.” You will see both. In your formal research writing, use the plural form unless your instructor or style guide says otherwise. Data Need Context Data are only meaningful when we understand what they represent. For example, a value of 75 does not mean much by itself. It could be a test score, heart rate, temperature, percentile rank, or something else entirely. To interpret data correctly, we need to know what each variable represents, how it was measured, and what the values mean. This is why clear variable names and documentation are important when working with datasets. How to Read and Work With Data To understand and use a dataset effectively, it’s important to recognize how data are organized. Well-structured datasets follow a few key principles: Rows represent observations (e.g., one participant, one trial, or one case) Columns represent variables (e.g., age, condition, score) Each cell contains a single value for one observation on one variable Variable names are clear and consistent, so you know what each column represents Missing data are clearly labeled (e.g., NA), rather than left blank, although jamovi knows to treat blank cells as missing data Data are stored in a clean, rectangular format, making them easier to analyze Understanding this structure will help you read datasets, identify variables, and prepare your data for analysis in tools like jamovi. Following these principles also helps prevent errors and makes data easier to analyze, share, and reproduce (Broman & Woo, 2018). What Is a Variable? A variable is anything we measure or record that can vary across individuals or observations. Examples of variables include: Age Gender Test scores Survey responses Some variables describe characteristics of participants, while others represent outcomes we are interested in understanding. You will learn more about different types of variables in the next section. Types of Data Not all variables are the same. Broadly, variables fall into two categories: Categorical variables describe groups or categories (e.g., gender, major, treatment condition) Continuous/numerical variables represent quantities or amounts (e.g., age, height, test scores) Variables are sometimes coded numerically (e.g., 0 = no, 1 = yes), but these numbers are simply labels. Coding a variable with numbers does not make it a continuous variable. Understanding the type of data you are working with will help you decide how to summarize and analyze it. In the next section, we will look more closely at different types of variables and what they allow us to do in statistical analysis. Why Do We Describe Data? Once we collect data, the first step is to describe what we have. Raw data (a spreadsheet full of numbers) can be difficult to interpret on their own. Descriptive statistics help us: Summarize large amounts of data Identify patterns or trends Understand what is typical or unusual Prepare for further analysis What Does It Mean to Describe Data? When we describe data, we are typically trying to understand three key features: Center (What is typical?): This tells us what a “typical” value looks like, often summarized using measures like the mean or median. These are called measures of central tendency. Variability (How spread out are the values?): This tells us how much the data differ from one another. Some datasets are tightly clustered, while others are more spread out. These are called measures of dispersion. Shape (What does the distribution look like?): This refers to how the data are distributed—for example, whether values are evenly spread out or skewed in one direction. You will learn how to calculate and interpret these in later chapters. Types of Descriptive Information There are two main ways we describe data: Numerical summaries, using measures of central tendency (e.g., mean, median, mode) and dispersion (e.g., standard deviation, variance). Visual summaries, using graphs and charts (e.g., histograms, bar charts) which help us quickly see patterns and differences. Looking Ahead Describing data is always the first step in any statistical analysis. You will use these ideas when you: Summarize your data (Chapter 4) Visualize patterns (Chapter 5) Begin hypothesis testing (Chapter 7) Interpret results from statistical tests (Chapters 11–14) Understanding your data before analyzing it will help you make better decisions and avoid mistakes. Key Takeaways Data are organized into rows (observations) and columns (variables) Variables are characteristics or outcomes that can vary Describing data involves understanding center, variability, and shape Descriptive statistics summarize and simplify data Describing data is the first step in any analysis Check Your Understanding By the end of this section, you should be able to: In a dataset, what does each row represent? What does each column represent? Give an example of a categorical variable and a continuous variable. Why is it important that each cell contains only one value? What are two features of a well-organized dataset? Why do data need context to be meaningful? Answers Rows represent observations (e.g., participants), and columns represent variables. Categorical: major. Continuous: age.
2.2 Types of Variables and Levels of Measurement

2.2 Types of Variables and Levels of Measurement In Section 2.1, we introduced two broad types of variables: categorical and continuous. We can refine this further into four levels of measurement. These levels help us understand what the values represent and what kinds of summaries and analyses are appropriate. Understanding the type of variable you are working with will help you decide: How to summarize the data What graphs to use What statistical analyses are appropriate Categorical Variables: Nominal and Ordinal Categorical variables describe groups or categories. There are two main types of categorical variables: nominal and ordinal variables. Nominal variables represent categories with no inherent order. There is a special type of nominal variable with only two categories (e.g., yes/no, present/absent), which we call a dichotomous variable. Examples: Major (Psychology, Business, Engineering) Treatment group (Control, Experimental) Eye color With nominal variables, we can: Count how many observations fall into each category Calculate percentages We cannot meaningfully order or average these values. Ordinal variables represent categories with a meaningful order, but the differences between values are not necessarily equal. Examples: Class rank (1st, 2nd, 3rd) Satisfaction ratings (Very dissatisfied → Very satisfied) Likert-scale responses (Strongly disagree → Strongly agree) With ordinal variables, we can: Order values Identify medians or rankings However, we should be cautious about treating the differences between values as equal. Also, keep in mind that assigning numbers to categories (e.g., 1 = strongly disagree, 5 = strongly agree) does not automatically make a variable continuous. Always consider what the values represent. Continuous (Numerical) Variables: Interval and Ratio Continuous variables, also called numerical variables, represent quantities or amounts. There are two main types of numerical variables: interval and ratio variables. Interval variables have equal spacing between values, but no true zero point. This means that differences between values are meaningful, but zero does not represent the absence of the construct. Examples: Temperature (°C or °F) Standardized test scores (e.g., IQ scores) Some psychological scale scores where zero is arbitrary With interval variables, we can: Compare differences between values (e.g., the difference between 90 and 100 is the same as between 100 and 110) Calculate means and standard deviations Create graphs and use many common statistical analyses However, we should not interpret ratios (e.g., a score of 120 is not “twice as much” as a score of 60). Ratio variables have equal spacing between values and a true zero point, meaning that zero represents the absence of the measured quantity. Examples: Reaction time (milliseconds) Number of correct responses on a task Time spent studying (minutes or hours) Frequency of a behavior (e.g., number of times a behavior occurs) With ratio variables, we can: Compute means and standard deviations Compare differences between values Make meaningful ratio statements (e.g., twice as much, half as much) Use a full range of statistical analyses Because ratio variables include a true zero, they provide the most flexibility for analysis. Discrete vs Count Variables Some continuous variables are measured on a scale (e.g., height, reaction time), while others are counts (e.g., number of correct responses). More specifically: Discrete variables take on countable values (e.g., number of children, number of errors) Continuous variables can take on any value within a range (e.g., time, height, weight) In practice, statistical software like jamovi typically treats both types as continuous variables, and we will do the same in this course. How Variable Type Affects What You Can Do Different types of variables allow for different kinds of summaries: Nominal (categorical): counts and percentages Ordinal: rankings, medians Interval and ratio (numerical): means, standard deviations, and more advanced analyses This distinction will become especially important when we begin summarizing data and selecting statistical tests. A Simple Way to Identify Variable Type When you encounter a variable, ask: Is this describing categories or numbers? If categories, is there a meaningful order? If so, then it’s ordinal; if not, then it’s nominal. If numbers, does zero represent “none”? If so, then it’s ratio; if not, then it’s interval. These questions can help you quickly determine the type of variable you are working with. The Same Construct Can Be Measured in Different Ways If levels of measurement still feel confusing, this is one of the most important things to remember: A variable’s level of measurement depends on how it is measured, not just what it is. For example, one semester a student asked, “Isn’t time a continuous variable?” The answer is: it depends. If time is measured as pre vs. post, it is a nominal variable with two categories. If time is measured as month of the year, it is ordinal because the categories have a meaningful order. If time is measured as response time, exact date, or time remaining, it is typically continuous. So “time” is not automatically one type of variable. Its level of measurement depends on the way the researcher defines and records it. Here is another example. The construct studying could also be measured in different ways. Name Study_Continuous Study_Ordinal Study_Nominal Name (Character) Hours studied per day Amount of studying Studies every day? Jesus 5.0 A great deal Yes Nicky 4.5 A great deal Yes Bradford 3.2 A moderate amount Yes Sylvia 1.7 A small amount Yes Martha 0.2 Rarely Yes Lillian 0.0 Never No Trayvon 0.0 Never No Notice that the same general idea—studying—can be measured as: continuous: hours studied per day ordinal: amount of studying on an ordered scale nominal: whether the person studies every day or not This matters because different ways of measuring a variable preserve different amounts of information. In general, moving from continuous → ordinal → nominal reduces information. For example, if we turn hours studied into “low,” “medium,” and “high,” we lose the exact differences between people. For that reason, researchers usually want to avoid collapsing variables into categories unless there is a strong reason to do so. For example, a mean split or median split turns a continuous variable into categories such as “above average” and “below average.” This may seem convenient, but it removes information and can make analyses less precise. At the same time, “more information” does not always mean “better.” The best level of measurement depends on your research question and how the construct is most meaningfully measured. Finally, just because values are written as numbers does not make a variable continuous. If I code cat = 1, dog = 2, and frog = 3, that is still a nominal variable. The numbers are just labels. Why This Matters Understanding variable types helps you: Choose appropriate summaries and graphs Avoid common mistakes (e.g., averaging categories) Prepare for statistical analysis We will build on this foundation in later chapters when we learn how to summarize and analyze data. Check Your Understanding Is “favorite color” a nominal, ordinal, interval, or ratio variable? Is a 1–5 satisfaction rating scale nominal or ordinal? Why? What makes ratio variables different from interval variables? Why can’t we calculate a meaningful average for nominal data? Give one example of each type of variable (nominal, ordinal, interval, ratio). Answers Nominal Ordinal, because the responses are ordered but the spacing between values is not guaranteed to be equal Ratio variables have a true zero point; interval variables do not Because the categories do not have numerical meaning or order Answers will vary (e.g., nominal: major; ordinal: class rank; interval: temperature °C; ratio: height)

2.3 Descriptive vs inferential statistics There are two broad types of statistics that we use when working with data: descriptive statistics and inferential statistics. Descriptive Statistics Descriptive statistics are used to summarize, organize, and describe the data we have collected. Common ways we describe data include: Measures of central tendency (e.g., mean, median, mode) Measures of dispersion (e.g., range, standard deviation, variance) Shape of the distribution (e.g., skew, kurtosis) Visualizations (e.g., tables, graphs) Descriptive statistics focus only on the sample data in front of us. Inferential Statistics Inferential statistics are used to make conclusions about a population based on data collected from a sample. Because we usually cannot collect data from an entire population (due to time, cost, or access), we collect data from a sample and use statistical methods to make inferences about the larger group. A parameter describes a population (e.g., the true average test score of all students), while a statistic describes a sample (e.g., the average score of the students we measured). In research, we use statistics to estimate unknown population parameters. Common inferential statistics include: t-tests ANOVA correlation chi-square tests regression These methods help us answer questions like: “Is this difference meaningful, or could it have occurred by chance?” How They Work Together Descriptive and inferential statistics are closely connected: We use descriptive statistics to summarize our sample We use inferential statistics to generalize from the sample to the population In practice, we almost always use both, but not all studies involve inference. Some studies are purely descriptive, focusing only on accurately summarizing the data collected. However, even when conducting inferential statistics, we begin by describing our sample. An example This can feel abstract, so let’s walk through a simple example. Imagine we are conducting a study to examine whether watching Schitt’s Creek (a very good show, but not helpful for studying) versus watching video lessons on studying techniques (useful, but perhaps less exciting) affects test performance in UW–Stout students. Our population of interest is all UW–Stout students (approximately 10k students). However, it is not feasible to collect data from every student due to time, access, and logistical constraints. Instead, we collect data from a sample. For simplicity, we use students from two sections of an introductory psychology course (about 80 students). This is a convenience sample, not a random sample, but it works for illustrating our concepts. We randomly assign half of the students to watch Schitt’s Creek and the other half to watch study skills videos. One week later, all students take an exam, and we record their test scores. Step 1: Describing the Sample First, we use descriptive statistics to summarize the data. We might: Create a histogram of test scores (perhaps separated by group) Calculate the mean score for each group Examine the standard deviation to understand variability Check the range of scores These summaries help us understand what the data look like. Step 2: Making Inferences Next, we ask our research question: Which group performed better on the exam? To answer this, we use inferential statistics. We take our descriptive statistics (means, standard deviations, sample sizes) and use a statistical test (in this case, an independent samples t-test) to evaluate whether the difference between groups is meaningful. Rather than calculating this by hand, we typically use statistical software like jamovi, which provides a test statistic, a p-value, and an effect size. These results help us determine whether the observed difference in our sample likely reflects a real difference in the population. Why This Matters Understanding the difference between descriptive and inferential statistics helps you: Clearly describe your data Ask meaningful research questions Make appropriate conclusions about a population Avoid overgeneralizing from limited data We will build on these ideas throughout the rest of the course, especially when we begin conducting inferential tests. Check Your Understanding You collect data from 50 UW–Stout students and calculate the mean GPA. Is this an example of descriptive or inferential statistics? Explain briefly. You use those 50 students to test whether UW–Stout students have a higher GPA than the national average. Is this descriptive or inferential statistics? Explain briefly. A researcher reports that 62% of participants preferred online classes. What type of statistic is this? Why do we typically use both descriptive and inferential statistics in the same study? A study includes only descriptive statistics and no inferential tests. Give one reason why that might be appropriate. Answers Descriptive. You are summarizing the sample you collected. Inferential. You are using the sample to make a claim about a larger population. Descriptive. It summarizes the sample data. Descriptive statistics summarize what the sample looks like, and inferential statistics help us decide what the sample may suggest about the population. Answers will vary. For example, the goal of the study may simply be to describe a group or summarize observations without making broader population claims.

2.4 Understanding Distributions and Variability So far, we have discussed what data are, how variables are measured, and the difference between descriptive and inferential statistics. Before we begin calculating statistics, it is important to understand what data look like as a whole. When we collect data, we do not just have individual values; we have a distribution. What is a Distribution? A distribution shows how values in a dataset are spread out and how often they occur. Rather than focusing on a single value, distributions help us see patterns across all observations. Key Features of a Distribution When we examine a distribution, there are a few key features to consider: Center: Where do most values fall? Variability: How spread out are the values? Shape: What pattern do the values form? Outliers: Are there unusually high or low values? These features (center, variability, shape, and outliers) are what we will later summarize using descriptive statistics such as the mean and standard deviation. Shape of a Distribution Distributions can take different shapes, and these shapes help us understand the data. One particular distribution we care a lot about in psychological research is the normal distribution, which is a specific type of symmetrical distribution where: Most values cluster around the center Fewer values appear as you move away from the center The left and right sides are approximately equal Many statistical methods assume that data are approximately normally distributed, which is why this shape is especially important. Skewed Distributions However, not all data are normally distributed. In a skewed distribution, one side of the distribution stretches out more than the other. Right-skewed (positive skew): tail extends to the right Left-skewed (negative skew): tail extends to the left Skew affects how we interpret the center of the data and which summary statistics are most appropriate. Kurtosis (Shape of the Tails) Another aspect of shape is kurtosis, which describes how heavy or light the tails of a distribution are. Some distributions have more extreme values (heavier tails) Others have fewer extreme values (lighter tails) For now, it is enough to recognize that distributions can differ not just in symmetry, but also in how extreme values appear. Variability of a Distribution Variability refers to how much the values in a dataset differ from one another. Two datasets can have the same center but very different variability. We will later quantify variability using measures such as: Variance Standard deviation For now, focus on the idea that variability tells us how consistent or spread out the data are. Outliers An outlier is a value that is unusually far from the rest of the data. Outliers can: Affect the shape of a distribution (e.g., creating skew) Increase variability Influence summary statistics like the mean (but, notably, not the median) Because of their impact, it is important to identify and think carefully about outliers when analyzing data. Important note: Not all outliers are errors. Some may represent meaningful or important observations, so it is important to examine them carefully before deciding how to handle them. This will be discussed later in Chapter 6 (Cleaning data). Why This Matters Understanding distributions helps you: Interpret summary statistics like the mean and standard deviation Recognize patterns and unusual values Choose appropriate analyses Avoid misleading conclusions In the next chapters, we will learn how to: Describe distributions numerically (Chapter 4) Visualize distributions using graphs (Chapter 5) Prepare and clean data for analysis (Chapter 6) A Quick Preview When working with data, you will often ask: Where is the center of the data? How spread out are the values? What shape does the distribution have? Are there any unusual values? These questions form the foundation of descriptive statistics. Check Your Understanding What is a distribution? What are the four key features of a distribution? What is a normal distribution? How do outliers affect a dataset? Why can two datasets with the same mean still be very different? Answers A distribution shows how values are spread out and how often they occur Center, variability, shape, and outliers A symmetrical distribution where most values cluster around the center They can affect shape, variability, and summary statistics Because they may differ in variability or shape

2.5 Putting It All Together In this chapter, we introduced the foundational ideas needed to understand and work with data. These concepts will be used throughout the rest of the course and serve as a reference point as you learn new statistical techniques. Big Picture Summary Data are values collected to answer questions A dataset is made up of cases (observations) and variables Variables can be categorical or continuous, and how a variable is measured affects what we can do with it Descriptive statistics summarize the data we observe Inferential statistics allow us to make conclusions about a population based on a sample Data form distributions, which can be described by their center, variability, shape, and outliers How These Concepts Connect These ideas are not separate—they work together as part of a process. You can think of working with data as a sequence: We begin by defining variables and collecting data These data form a dataset When we examine all values together, we see a distribution We use descriptive statistics to summarize that distribution We use inferential statistics to answer research questions about a population Understanding how these pieces fit together will make the rest of the course much easier. Describes typical value Variability How spread out are values? Shows consistency Shape What pattern do values form? Affects interpretation Outliers Are there unusual values? Can influence results Key Ideas to Remember A variable’s level of measurement depends on how it is measured, not just what it represents Assigning numbers to categories does not make a variable continuous Collapsing variables into categories can reduce information Distributions provide a complete picture of the data, not just a single value Both descriptive and inferential statistics are typically used together Looking Ahead In the next chapters, you will begin working directly with data: Chapter 3: Getting started with jamovi Chapter 4: Describing data using statistics Chapter 5: Visualizing data Chapter 6: Cleaning and preparing data Each of these chapters will build directly on the concepts introduced here. A variable is coded as: 1 = cat 2 = dog 3 = frog Why is this still not a continuous variable? Answers Major → categorical → frequencies or percentages Stress rating → ordinal (categorical) → median or frequencies Hours of sleep → continuous → mean and standard deviation Variable type determines what descriptive statistics, graphs, and analyses are appropriate. If a variable is classified incorrectly, the results may be misleading. Variability, shape, range, and possibly sample size. The mean alone does not fully describe the data. Because the numbers are only labels for categories; they do not represent meaningful numerical quantities.
