3. Overview of jamovi In this chapter, we begin working with statistical software: jamovi. The goal of this chapter is not to perform statistical analyses yet. Instead, we will focus on becoming familiar with jamovi, understanding how data are organized in the software, and learning some basic tasks that will prepare us for later chapters. The concepts introduced in Chapter 2—such as variables, datasets, and levels of measurement—are directly applied here. As you work through this chapter, you will start to see how those ideas appear in practice.

3.1 What is jamovi? jamovi is a free, open-source statistical software program that allows you to: Organize and manage data Perform statistical analyses Create tables and visualizations Why are we using jamovi? There are several reasons we use jamovi in this course: It’s free.: You will never have to pay to use jamovi. This means you can continue using it in future courses, research projects, or your career. There is an optional paid version of jamovi Cloud, but it is not required. It’s open source.: jamovi is developed and maintained by the statistical community. As the developers describe it, “jamovi is made by the scientific community, for the scientific community.” This means the software is continuously improving and grounded in current best practices. It’s built on R.: jamovi is built on top of the R statistical programming language. If you are interested in learning to code, jamovi provides a natural entry point. Many researchers (including me) use R in environments like RStudio for more advanced analyses. In fact, this textbook was created using RStudio and hosted on GitHub. It’s easy to learn and use.: Compared to other statistical software (e.g., SPSS), jamovi is much more intuitive. Students typically find it easier to learn and prefer using it for their analyses. It supports reproducibility.: jamovi saves your data, analyses, settings, and results all in one file. This makes it easy to return to your work, check what you did, and reproduce your results—an important part of good scientific practice. And if you don’t want to hear it from me, here’s another person’s description of what jamovi is and why it’s a great alternative to SPSS that provides a useful path toward learning R. Additional jamovi videos and resources Although this textbook aims to serve all your basic jamovi and statistical needs, here are some additional resources on jamovi that I recommend you bookmark and use when you are struggling or need additional support: jamovi 2022 tutorials by Alexander Swan on YouTube jamovi docs provide more support for using jamovi, including tips for transitioning from SPSS to jamovi and from jamovi to R Introduction to jamovi LinkedIn Learning course by Barton Poulson, founder of datalab.cc (you can find the video on YouTube and other places now, too, if you do not have LinkedIn Learning) [Caution: this course uses an older version of jamovi] practice resources and datasets for additional hands-on work by Neumann, Hood, & Neumann (2013)! Keep in mind that some tutorials may use older versions of jamovi, so the menus or layout may look slightly different from what you see on your screen.

3.2 Installing jamovi To install jamovi, go to the download page. I recommend downloading the current version with the latest features, but you may want to get the solid version if you prefer something more stable. jamovi has more documentation on how to install jamovi per operating system. Installation on macOS and Windows should be pretty straightforward. A few notes: Here’s some guidance if you are getting an error on Windows that “The app you’re trying to install isn’t a Microsoft verified app.” Chromebooks can be trickier. If your Chromebook does not use Linux, you may have difficulty installing jamovi. If so, look for Chromebook-specific installation support, such as this video on installing jamovi on a Chromebook. If you are unable to install jamovi right away, do not panic. Contact the instructor or teaching assistant for support. The most important thing is to start getting familiar with the software and how it is organized.

3.3 Opening data in jamovi Before we can analyze data, we need to get it into jamovi and make sure it is set up correctly. You can open existing datasets in jamovi, including files such as Excel spreadsheets and SPSS files. When you open a file, take a moment to look through the data and ask: Are the rows and columns organized the way I expect? Are the variables labeled clearly? Are the variable types set correctly? Are there missing values or unusual entries? That quick first look can save you a lot of problems later. Alexander Swan has a video on opening files in jamovi. Opening SPSS (.sav) files in jamovi If you are working with SPSS files (with a .sav file extension), jamovi can usually open them directly. This can be especially useful if you are working with older datasets or files shared by others. Alexander Swan has an additional video on opening .sav files in jamovi. Entering data manually in jamovi Sometimes you will need to enter data manually rather than opening an existing file. If you are entering data manually: make sure your variable names are clear make sure each row is a case and each column is a variable make sure variable types are set correctly enter data consistently This is also a good time to apply what we learned in Chapter 2 about good datasets and spreadsheets. Alexander Swan has yet another video on entering data manually in jamovi. Annotating output in jamovi Sometimes you may need to submit output files from jamovi with annotations. This can be helpful for showing your work, identifying key results, or explaining how you interpreted an analysis. If you have not done this before, it is worth reviewing a short tutorial by Alexander Swan on annotating output in jamovi.

3.4 Navigating the jamovi interface Once you open jamovi, you will see a spreadsheet-like layout. This is where your data will appear. Like we discussed in Chapter 2: each row represents a case or observation each column represents a variable This structure is important to keep in mind, because many problems in statistical analysis begin with incorrectly structured or incorrectly labeled data. I recommend watching a short video by Alexander Swan on navigating the jamovi interface in jamovi so you can become familiar with the layout and the basic menus. Note that some demonstrations you find online may use a Mac and may include extra modules, so the screen may look a little different from yours. Understanding variable setup in jamovi Variable Name First, you should use an informative yet brief variable name. Refer back to Chapter 2 for tips on variable names from Broman & Woo (2018). Variable Description Second, you should provide a longer description of the variable. You could provide the full text of the item in the survey, more details on how the variable was measured, or anything else that can help you or others better understand the variable. Measure type One of the most important parts of working in jamovi is correctly identifying your variable types. In jamovi, variables are set as one of four measure types: Nominal: categories with no order Ordinal: categories with a meaningful order Continuous: numerical variables ID: an identifier variable that you would not usually analyze, such as participant ID Data type Additionally, variables can be one of three data types: Integer, meaning the values are discrete whole numbers Decimal, meaning the values are numbers with decimals Text, meaning the values are alphanumeric, not just numeric These levels of measurement and data types connect directly to the ideas from Chapter 2. A variable may be coded numerically (for example, 0 = no and 1 = yes), but that does not mean it is continuous. You still need to specify the correct variable type. Missing values You can specify which values are intended to be missing values. For example, -99 is commonly defined as a missing value in a dataset. Levels For categorical variables, you can specify that text that aligns with numeric values of a variable. For example, if you have a dataset in which pretest is coded as 1 and posttest is coded as 2, you can specify this in the Levels box. There is a toggle you can select to retain unused levels in analyses if you want any level that has no data to always show up (e.g., in a graph). Check Your Understanding A variable is coded as: 0 = No 1 = Yes Should this variable be set as continuous, ordinal, or nominal in jamovi? Why? You open a dataset and jamovi labels a variable as continuous, but it represents categories such as: 1 = Freshman 2 = Sophomore 3 = Junior 4 = Senior What should you do? In an independent samples t-test: What type of variable should go in the dependent variable box? What type of variable should go in the grouping variable box? Why is jamovi’s automatic detection of variable type helpful, but not enough on its own? A student says, “If jamovi lets me run the analysis, it must be the right one.” What is wrong with that reasoning? Answers Nominal. The numbers are labels for categories, not continuous quantities. Change the variable type to ordinal, because the categories have a meaningful order. Dependent variable → continuous Grouping variable → categorical (nominal or ordinal) jamovi’s automatic detection is helpful because it saves time, but it can make mistakes. You still need to confirm that the variable type matches what the variable actually represents. jamovi can help guide you, but it is not a substitute for statistical reasoning. A variable can be set incorrectly, and software cannot always determine whether the analysis truly matches the research question. How jamovi helps you work with variables Once you understand how variables are defined, it’s important to understand how jamovi helps you work with them. jamovi will try to automatically determine variable types. When you enter data or open a dataset, jamovi will guess the data type and measure type for you. This is incredibly helpful—until it’s wrong. Always double-check your variables before running any analyses. Incorrect variable types are one of the most common sources of errors in statistical work. The icons help guide your analysis choices. Those small icons next to variables are not just decorative—they tell you what kind of variable you are working with and where it can be used. For example: A ruler icon indicates a continuous variable A Venn icon indicates a nominal variable A histogram icon indicates an ordinal variable These icons also appear in analysis menus to guide you. jamovi helps prevent (some) mistakes—but not all. When you run analyses, jamovi will often indicate what types of variables belong in each box. For example, in an independent samples t-test: The dependent variable box shows a ruler icon → you should enter a continuous variable The grouping variable box shows a Venn diagram or historgram → you should enter a nominal or ordinal variable This is incredibly helpful—but you still need to understand why those choices matter. jamovi is a guide, not a substitute for statistical reasoning. Most common mistake Running an analysis with incorrectly specified variables. Always check: Variable type (nominal, ordinal, continuous) Data type (numeric vs text) Whether the variable fits the analysis A first look at your data After loading or entering your data, your next step is to quickly check that your dataset is ready for analysis. At this stage, that does not mean running formal statistical tests. Instead, it means making sure your data are set up correctly. As you look through your data, consider: Are the variables named clearly? Are the variable types correct? Are there obvious data entry mistakes? Are there missing values that aren’t coded as missing? Are there values that seem impossible or unusual? We will learn more about cleaning and preparing data in Chapter 6, but building this habit now will make your analyses much smoother. If something goes wrong in your analysis later, the first place to check is your variable setup. Check Your Understanding You see a value of 999 in a test score variable where scores should range from 0 to 100. What should you do before analyzing the data? Why is it important to look through your dataset before running analyses? You notice that a variable contains the values: Yes yes Y Why might this be a problem? If something looks wrong in your analysis output later, what is one of the first things you should check? Answers Investigate the value before analyzing the data. It may be a data entry error, a missing value code, or some other issue. Looking through the dataset helps you catch mistakes, incorrect variable types, missing values, and unusual or impossible values before they affect your results. jamovi may treat these as different categories, which would distort frequencies and percentages. Check your variable setup and the raw data values first.

4. Describing data in jamovi In this chapter, we begin describing our data using statistical summaries. In Chapter 2, we learned what descriptive statistics are and why they matter. In Chapter 3, we learned how to set up and navigate data in jamovi. Now, we will bring those together and begin using jamovi to describe our data. By the end of this chapter, you should be able to: Generate descriptive statistics in jamovi Choose appropriate descriptive statistics based on variable type Interpret measures of center and variability Write up descriptive statistics clearly

4.1 Describing continuous variables We will start by describing continuous variables, such as test scores, age, or reaction time. Running descriptive statistics in jamovi To generate descriptive statistics: Click Exploration Select Descriptives Move your variable into the Variables box Once you do this, jamovi will automatically generate output. In the options panel, you can select which statistics to display. For most purposes, you should include: Mean: the average value Median: the middlemost value Standard deviation: average spread around the mean Variance: squared spread Minimum and maximum: the lowest and highest values, respectively N (sample size): the total number of observations You may also include: Skewness: the shape of the tail of the distribution Kurtosis: the shape of the height of the distribution Interpreting the output Once you run the analysis, jamovi will provide a table of descriptive statistics. When interpreting a continuous variable, focus on: Center such as mean or median Variability such as standard deviation or variance Range such as the highest and lowest values You can ask yourself the following questions to understand the distribution of the data: Are the mean and median similar, which suggests a more normal distribution? Are the values tightly clustered or widely spread out? Are there potential outliers? Do the highest and lowest values make sense based on the dataset? This information is often used to write-up results in APA Style. For example: The average test score was 78.4 (SD = 10.2), with scores ranging from 52 to 95. This tells us: the typical score (mean) how spread out scores are (SD) the range of observed values I recommend you watch this video by Alexander Swan on how to describe continuous data in jamovi. Check Your Understanding You run descriptive statistics and get: Mean = 75 Median = 60 What might this suggest about the distribution? Why is it important to report both the mean and the standard deviation? Two datasets both have a mean of 80. One has a standard deviation of 2 and the other has a standard deviation of 15. What does this tell you? A variable has a minimum of 18 and a maximum of 19, but the standard deviation is very large. What would you want to check? If the mean and median are very different, which measure of center might be more useful to report? Why? Answers The distribution may be skewed. Because the mean is greater than the median, it may be right-skewed. The mean describes the center of the data, while the standard deviation describes how spread out the values are. Both are needed to understand the distribution. The two datasets have the same average, but the second dataset is much more variable. You would want to check the raw data and output carefully, because those values may not be consistent. There could be an error or a misunderstanding in the output. The median may be more useful, because it is less affected by skew and outliers.

4.2 Describing categorical variables Now let’s describe categorical variables, such as gender, major, or condition. Running frequencies in jamovi To describe categorical variables: Click Exploration Select Descriptives Move your variable into the Variables box Select the Frequency tables check box Interpreting output Example: Category Count Percent Yes 45 56.3% No 35 43.8% Interpretation: 56.3% of participants responded “Yes,” while 43.8% responded “No.” Key reminder For categorical variables: We do not calculate means or standard deviations We describe how often each category occurs A statistics table will be provided, but as the variable is categorical it will only provide N and Missing, as the other statistics are used for continuous variables I recommend you watch this video by Alexander Swan on how to describe categorical data in jamovi. Check Your Understanding Why would it be inappropriate to calculate the mean for a nominal variable? What statistics are typically most appropriate for categorical variables? A variable has the categories: Psychology Business Engineering What would be a useful way to describe this variable? A student codes major as: 1 = Psychology 2 = Business 3 = Engineering They then calculate the mean and report that the “average major” was 2.1. What is the problem with this interpretation? Answers Nominal variables represent categories, not numerical quantities, so averages are not meaningful. Frequencies (counts) and percentages. Report how many participants fall into each category and the percentage in each category. The numbers are only codes for categories. They do not represent meaningful numerical values, so the mean is not interpretable.
4.3 Describing a continuous variable by a categorical variable

4.3 Describing a continuous variable by a categorical variable Often, we want to describe a continuous variable across groups. Example: Test scores by condition Stress levels by major Running grouped descriptives In Descriptives: Click Exploration Select Descriptives Place your continuous variable in the Variables box Place your categorical variable in the Split by box Interpreting output jamovi will now provide descriptive statistics for each group separately. This allows you to compare: group means group variability sample sizes For example: Students in the intervention group scored higher (M = 82.3, SD = 8.5) than students in the control group (M = 75.6, SD = 11.2). Descriptive statistics are not inferential statistics. We can describe differences—but we are not yet testing whether those differences are statistically significant. That comes later. Be careful that you write up descriptive statistics as simply describing the data and not making causal inferences. Check Your Understanding You compare test scores between two groups and find: Group A: M = 80, SD = 8 Group B: M = 75, SD = 10 Can you conclude that the difference is statistically significant? Why or why not? What does using the Split by box in jamovi allow you to examine? Why might it be useful to compare both the means and standard deviations across groups? A researcher reports that Group A scored higher than Group B descriptively. What additional step would be needed to test whether that difference is statistically significant? Answers No. These are descriptive statistics only. You would need an inferential test to evaluate statistical significance. It allows you to describe a continuous variable separately across the levels of a categorical variable. The means show differences in center, and the standard deviations show differences in variability. Conduct an inferential test, such as an independent samples t-test, if appropriate.

4.4 Choosing the right descriptive statistics Not all statistics are appropriate for all variables. Here is a quick reminder from Chapter 2. The type of variable determines: what statistics you can compute how you interpret the results This is why correctly setting your variable type in jamovi (Chapter 3) is so important. Variable Type What to Report Continuous Mean, median, SD, range Ordinal Median, frequencies Nominal Frequencies, percentages Check Your Understanding Which descriptive statistics would be most appropriate for each of the following? Favorite color Class rank Reaction time Why might the median be preferred over the mean for some variables? A variable is ordinal, but a researcher reports the mean. What question should you ask before deciding whether that is appropriate? Why is the type of variable so important when deciding how to describe data? Answers Favorite color → frequencies and percentages Class rank → median or frequencies Reaction time → mean, standard deviation, range The median is less affected by skew and outliers. Ask whether the variable is being treated as approximately continuous and whether that choice is conceptually justified. Variable type determines which summaries are meaningful and appropriate.

4.5 Writing up descriptive statistics Being able to compute statistics is not enough—you also need to communicate them clearly. Basic format (continuous variable) M = , SD = Example: Participants reported moderate stress levels (M = 3.45, SD = 0.82). Including range Scores ranged from ___ to ___ Group comparisons Group A (M = , SD = ) scored higher/lower than Group B (M = , SD = ). Tips Always include units (e.g., test scores, minutes, ratings) Round consistently (typically 2 decimal places) Be clear and concise Examples from the literature In small examples, we might write-up our descriptive statistics into a paragraph1 (note: I also describe an independent t-test and a chi-square test of independence in this paragraph): In examples with many variables, we might write-up our descriptive statistics into a table2: Check Your Understanding Rewrite this more clearly: “The average score was 78 and spread was 10.” What important information is missing from this write-up? “Participants in Group A scored higher than participants in Group B.” Why is it important to include units or context when writing up descriptive statistics? A student writes: “The mean major was 2.3.” What feedback would you give? Answers Example: “Participants had an average score of 78 (SD = 10).” The means, standard deviations, and likely sample sizes for each group are missing. Numbers alone can be unclear. Context helps readers understand what the values represent. Major is a categorical variable, so the mean is not meaningful. They should report frequencies or percentages instead. This comes from Wanzer (2017) Developmentally appropriate evaluations: How evaluation practices differ across age of participants↩︎ This comes from Wanzer et al. (2020) Experiencing flow while viewing art: Development of the aesthetic experience questionnaire↩︎

4.6 Common mistakes Here are a few common mistakes to avoid: Reporting means for categorical variables Ignoring variability (only reporting the mean) Misinterpreting skewed data Forgetting to report sample size Not checking your data before analysis Check Your Understanding What is one problem with reporting only the mean for a continuous variable? Why is it a mistake to report means for nominal variables? If a variable is coded incorrectly in jamovi, how might that affect your descriptive statistics? A researcher finds a very large standard deviation. What are two possible explanations? Answers The mean alone does not show variability, skew, or possible outliers. Nominal variables are categories, not quantities, so a mean is not interpretable. jamovi may calculate inappropriate statistics or allow the wrong type of analysis. The data may truly be very spread out, or there may be outliers, errors, or incorrect coding.

4.7 Looking ahead In this chapter, we focused on summarizing data numerically. In the next chapter, we will learn how to visualize data using graphs, which can often reveal patterns that numbers alone cannot. Together, descriptive statistics and visualizations provide a complete picture of your data.

5. Visualizing data in jamovi In Chapter 4, we described data using numerical summaries such as means and standard deviations. In this chapter, we will describe data using visualizations. Graphs allow us to see patterns in our data that may not be obvious from numbers alone. They are an essential part of understanding and communicating statistical results. By the end of this chapter, you should be able to: - Choose an appropriate graph based on variable type - Create visualizations in jamovi - Interpret what a graph shows - Identify common issues in graphs

5.1 Why visualize data? In Chapter 4, we described data using numbers such as means, standard deviations, and percentages. While these summaries are essential, they do not always tell the full story. You have likely heard the phrase, “a picture is worth a thousand words.” This is especially true in statistics. A well-designed graph can often communicate patterns in data more quickly and clearly than a table of numbers or a written paragraph. Visualizations can help you: See the shape of a distribution (e.g., symmetric or skewed) Identify outliers or unusual values Understand variability and spread Detect patterns or trends that are not obvious from numerical summaries For example, two datasets can have the same mean and standard deviation but look very different when graphed. Without a visualization, those differences might go unnoticed. Visualizations in practice Visualizations are not just helpful for your own understanding—they are also essential for communicating results to others. In research and professional settings: Journal articles often have word or page limits Reports need to be clear and efficient Audiences may not have strong statistical backgrounds A single well-designed graph can: summarize large amounts of information highlight key findings make results more accessible to a broader audience Because of this, visualizations are commonly used alongside (or sometimes instead of) detailed numerical summaries. Key takeaway Descriptive statistics and visualizations work together: Statistics provide precise numerical summaries Visualizations provide an overall picture of the data Using both allows you to better understand and communicate your results. Remember: it is incredibly important to always visualize your data! You never know what descriptive statistics may be hiding. Here’s a video walking through why it’s so important to look at your data.

5.2 Choosing the right graph The type of graph you use depends on the type of variable(s) you are working with. Variable Type Graph Continuous Histogram, density plot, boxplot Categorical Bar chart Continuous + categorical Bar chart, line chart, boxplot Continuous + continuous Scatter plot, line chart Choosing the correct graph is just as important as choosing the correct statistical test. Want to learn more about choosing the right chart? Chart Chooser provides a useful tutorial for choosing charts based on your variable type and objective. jamovi has some plots built into its platform, both under the Plots drop-down menu in the Descriptives analysis and as options for many of the inferential statistical analyses. We’ll learn more about how to choose and conduct better data visualizations later, but for now here are some recommended visualizations depending on what you are trying to do. Note that we will do most of our visualizations in jamovi, but we may also learn how to visualize data via Excel. There are also excellent LinkedIn Learning courses on data visualization in Excel and other tools that I strongly recommend; they are free for you if you are a UW-Stout student.

5.1 Visualizing a single continuous variable As described above, a histogram and/or density plot is one of the best ways to visualize a single continuous variable. Both are useful for seeing the overall distribution of your data and to help check for normality. Which should you use? I think they’re both pretty great, and in fact you can combine the two to have a histogram plot with a density overlay. I like this option best because it presents more information and better lets us see if the if the density curve looks normally distributed. Creating a histogram in jamovi Go to Exploration → Descriptives Move your variable into the Variables box Under Plots, select Histogram and/or Density When interpreting a histogram or density plot, look for: Shape: Is the distribution symmetric or skewed? Center: Where are most values located? Variability: How spread out are the values? Outliers: Are there unusually high or low values? Histograms help you visually understand: - mean and median (center) - standard deviation (spread) - skewness (shape)
5.2 A categorical variable For this you would choose the single option under Bar Plots: Bar plot. It will simply show the frequencies of a categorical variable.
5.3 A continuous variable split by a categorical variable

5.3 A continuous variable split by a categorical variable There are three options under Box Plots: Box plot, Violin (which is really a density plot with its mirror image!), Data (which can be Jittered or Stacked; I prefer Jittered so you can see the density of data points really well), and Mean. Personally, I love checking all four boxes! This gives you the best of all of them: the distribution of your data with the Violin option, the quartiles and mean with the Box plot option, a visualization of all your data points using the Data option, which is really useful because the other two options can be hiding weird things in your data, and what the Mean is.

5.4 Expanding your data visualization Although these can be useful plots, I often do most of my data visualizations in other platforms. For most of my work, I use Excel because I find it pretty easy to make beautiful graphs. Here’s an example of a visualization I made in Excel3: For some more complicated figures, I turn to the ggplot2 package in R.
